title: Warsh Rewrites the Fed Playbook, Iran Deal Reshapes Oil Markets, and Russia Cuts Cautiously
subhead: The Warsh era at the Fed begins with forward guidance scrapped and rate-hike speculation rising. The US-Iran MoU unlocks oil sales and a $300B fund but leaves the hardest questions for later. Russia cuts rates by less than expected, and gold heads for a third weekly loss.

sections.heading: Warsh Kills Forward Guidance, Launches Fed Overhaul
sections.body:
Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting as Fed chair didn't change rates — but it changed almost everything else. The new chair scrapped forward guidance, calling it 'not well suited' to the current moment, and skipped his own dot on the rate-path chart, signaling the Fed will no longer telegraph its next moves. He also launched five task forces to review the Fed's communications, inflation gauge, and decision-making framework.

Markets immediately repriced rate-hike odds upward. Former Fed Vice Chair Roger Ferguson told CNBC he still expects a rate hike this year under Warsh. The policy statement was shorter and deliberately vaguer than under Powell, and Warsh's press conference emphasized data dependence over predictability.

Investors are now bracing for a less predictable central bank. Reuters reported that Wall Street is adjusting to sharp moves as the Fed pulls back from signaling future rate paths. The Seeking Alpha analysis noted that Warsh 'killed the dot plot' and that forward guidance is gone, replaced by an 'element of surprise' approach.
sections.whyItMatters: The Fed's communication architecture — built over three decades — is being dismantled. Markets priced stability around predictable Fed signals; removing them raises volatility and shifts power back toward discretionary, less transparent decision-making.

sections.heading: Iran Deal: Oil Flows, $300B Fund, and 60 Days of Uncertainty
sections.body:
Iran published the full text of the US-Iran Memorandum of Understanding, kicking off a 60-day implementation window where commercial effects may arrive before the legal framework is finished. Vice President JD Vance confirmed the clock started Thursday.

The MoU has two tracks: an immediate one that lifts the US naval blockade of Iranian ports within 30 days and grants temporary waivers for Iranian oil sales, shipping, and insurance; and a deferred track where permanent sanctions relief, enrichment disposal, and compliance mechanisms are left for later negotiation.

According to Reuters, more than half of the proposed $300 billion reconstruction fund has already been committed by private-sector companies from the US, Gulf states, Asia, South America, and Africa. VP Vance insisted 'the United States isn't giving up a cent' and that the fund is a private investment vehicle, not a government program. Trump echoed this on Truth Social, calling reports of a US payment 'Fake News.'

The Wall Street Journal estimates the deal could allow Iran to earn more than $60 billion annually from resumed oil exports. But the Straits Times notes that Israel is deeply unsatisfied, and the deal folds Lebanon into the ceasefire framework — meaning multiple fronts could disrupt the arrangement.
sections.whyItMatters: The MoU trades immediate economic relief for deferred political guarantees. Iran gets oil revenue flowing within weeks; the US gets a temporary nuclear commitment backed by nothing permanent yet. The $300B fund — funded largely by private capital — is an unusual mechanism that creates commercial stakes in the deal's survival.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Slide to 3-Month Lows, But Recovery Will Be Gradual
sections.body:
Crude prices have fallen roughly 10% this week, with Brent and WTI settling near pre-war levels. Middle East crude has slipped into discounts for the first time since the conflict began, according to Reuters data, as the Hormuz reopening brightens the supply outlook. US gas prices dipped below $4 per gallon for the first time since March.

But the physical market tells a more cautious story. Only 26 vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, per maritime data firm Windward — far below the 138 daily transits typical before the war. Shipping companies are holding back due to unresolved mine risks and insurance concerns. Analysts tell TIME it could take weeks or months for traffic to normalize.

Spot premiums for crude and refined products in Asia, Europe, and Africa have fallen back toward pre-war levels, but Reuters notes that 'shipping angst provides a floor.' Iran has also signaled it may impose 'service fees' on transiting ships after the 60-day toll-free period — a move that would permanently raise transit costs on a waterway carrying a fifth of global oil.
sections.whyItMatters: The price signal is clear — oil is pricing in peace — but the physical market is far from normalized. Mine clearance, insurance, and Iran's potential toll regime mean the discount could be slow to reach consumers and the supply recovery uneven across regions.

sections.heading: Gold Heads for Third Weekly Loss as Hawkish Fed and Strong Dollar Weigh
sections.body:
Gold fell more than 2% on Friday, on track for a third consecutive weekly decline, pressured by a firmer US dollar and Warsh's hawkish debut. Spot gold dropped below $4,180 per ounce on Friday, down from over $4,240 the day before.

The dual pressure is notable: the Iran deal is reducing the geopolitical risk premium that had supported gold, while Warsh's Fed overhaul is strengthening the dollar and raising the specter of further rate hikes. Alexander Morris, CEO of F/m Investments, told CNBC that easing energy prices won't eliminate broader inflation pressures, and that gold will 'pivot back to an inflation hedge' once the deal-driven noise fades.

For now, the Warsh effect dominates. A hawkish Fed with no forward guidance means markets cannot discount future easing — and that is a structural headwind for gold.
sections.whyItMatters: Gold's three-week slide reflects two simultaneous regime shifts: geopolitical de-escalation reducing the risk bid, and a less predictable, more hawkish Fed strengthening the dollar. The inflation-hedge case for gold remains, but it's currently submerged under these crosscurrents.

sections.heading: Russia Cuts Rates Cautiously as War Economy Constraints Bite
sections.body:
The Bank of Russia cut its benchmark rate by just 25 basis points to 14.25% on Friday — half the 50 bps analysts expected — marking the ninth consecutive rate reduction. The central bank cited risks from soft budget policy and a decline in fuel production as reasons for caution.

Bloomberg reported that a stronger ruble — partly driven by the Iran deal's impact on oil markets — gave the bank room to cut, but the Wall Street Journal noted that Russia's economy contracted recently, adding urgency to easing. The bank signaled that further cuts would come at a slower pace, acknowledging the structural drag of high military spending on the Ukraine war.

The 25 bps move contrasts with the more aggressive easing cycle of the past year and suggests the central bank is running low on easy cuts. War spending continues to crowd out civilian investment, and fuel production shortfalls complicate the inflation outlook.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's smaller-than-expected cut signals that even with inflation easing, the war economy's structural constraints — massive defense spending and energy production declines — are limiting how far monetary policy can go. The easy part of the easing cycle is over.

sections.heading: ECB's Wunsch Keeps July Hike in Play Despite Falling Oil
sections.body:
ECB Governing Council member Pierre Wunsch told Reuters the European Central Bank may raise interest rates one more time as soon as July if it sees more evidence of euro zone inflation spreading beyond energy — even as the US-Iran deal pushes oil prices lower.

Wunsch's remarks are striking because cheaper oil would normally reduce the case for tightening. But the Belgian central bank governor argued that core inflation pressures persist and the ECB cannot assume the energy relief will fully transmit to broader price stability. The comment signals an ECB that remains in hawkish mode despite the geopolitical de-escalation.

This divergence — the Fed moving toward less predictability with possible hikes, the ECB explicitly keeping hikes on the table — creates a policy asymmetry that could weigh on the euro if oil-driven disinflation stalls before core inflation converges.
sections.whyItMatters: Wunsch's July-hike signal, delivered the same week oil dropped 10%, tells markets the ECB's inflation fight is not over just because energy got cheaper. If core stays sticky, Europe could tighten further even as the US becomes less transparent about its path.

sections.heading: US Holds Off Blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese Firms Ahead of Xi Meeting
sections.body:
The Trump administration delayed adding China's AI startup DeepSeek, memory chipmaker CXMT, and more than 100 other companies flagged as national security risks to the trade blacklist, according to two people familiar with the matter, as reported by Reuters. The decision was made to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing ahead of a meeting between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping.

The hold is significant: these companies had been identified by the Commerce Department as posing national security risks, but geopolitical considerations — specifically, managing the US-China relationship alongside the Iran deal and G7 diplomacy — overrode the security rationale, at least temporarily.

Separately at the G7, Canadian PM Mark Carney was overheard on a hot mic explaining Canada's China EV quota deal to Trump: a cap of 49,000 Chinese EVs per year at a reduced tariff rate, representing less than 3% of the Canadian market. Trump reportedly responded, 'That's good, I like it,' though he later qualified his remarks. The deal partially rolled back Canada's 100% tariff on Chinese EVs in exchange for China suspending retaliatory tariffs on Canadian farm goods. Carney suggested it could eventually lead to Chinese automakers building vehicles in Canada.
sections.whyItMatters: The DeepSeek hold and the Carney-Trump exchange reveal the current tension in US-China economic policy: security hawks want to restrict Chinese technology and trade, but the administration is selectively easing pressure when diplomacy requires it. The Entity List delay and the EV quota both suggest transactional flexibility over ideological consistency.

bottomLine: Three regime shifts converged this week. The Warsh Fed ended forward guidance and made US monetary policy less predictable just as the Iran deal started sending oil prices back toward pre-war levels. Russia's cautious 25 bps cut and the ECB's willingness to hike in July bookend a global monetary landscape that is anything but synchronized. Meanwhile, the US is selectively easing economic pressure on China — holding off on blacklisting DeepSeek ahead of the Trump-Xi meeting — while Iran gets immediate oil revenue in exchange for temporary nuclear commitments. The pattern is clear: short-term economic relief is being traded for long-term political uncertainty across every major theater.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, Wall Street Journal, Straits Times, TIME, USA Today, The Deep Dive, Seeking Alpha, Carscoops, Global News, Forbes, CNBC, MSN, Washington Post, New York Times, Gulf Magic, Financial Times
